perturbation.original_text: 1.1 “Agents ” means directors, officers, employees, agents, professi onal advisers, contractors or sub- contractors, or any Group member;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 “Representatives” means directors, officers, employees, agents, professional advisers, contractors or sub- contractors, or any Group member;
perturbation.explanation: The term 'Agents' has been changed to 'Representatives' in the definition section. This creates confusion because the term 'Agents' is used throughout the contract, for example, in clause 2.3. By redefining 'Agents' as 'Representatives' in this specific clause, the contract introduces ambiguity as to whether all instances of 'Agents' should now be interpreted as 'Representatives,' thus creating an in-text contradiction. The original term 'Agents' in clause 2.3 is expecting the original definition provided previously of 'Agents', and not the in-text definition change.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.3 Notwithstanding clause 2.1 above, the Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information: a) to those of its Agents who strictly need to know the Co nfidential Information for the sole purpose set out in clause 2.2 above, provided that the Receiving Party shall ensure that such Agents are made aware prior to the disclosure of an y part of the Confidential Information that the same is confidential and that they owe a duty of confidence to the Disclosing Party. The Receiving Party shall at all ti mes remain liable for any actions of such Agents that would constitute a breach of this Agreement ; or b) to the extent required by law or the rules of any applica ble regulatory authority, subject to clause 2.4 below.
perturbation.justification: The modification redefines 'Agents' to 'Representatives'. Clause 2.3 references 'Agents' and expects the original definition. By changing the definition, the contract introduces a contradiction as to whether all instances of 'Agents' should now be interpreted as 'Representatives'. This is a strong contradiction that could cause confusion and unenforceability because the contract language no longer aligns consistently with its own definitions. It creates a Misaligned Terminology which is a type of In Text Contradiction, per the Perturbation Definitions.

perturbation.original_text: 1.2 “Confidential Information ” means any information or other data relating to one p arty (the “Disclosing Party”) and/or the business carried on o r proposed or intended to be carried on by that party and which is made available for the purpos es of the Proposal to the other party (the “Receiving Party”) or its Agents by the Disclosing Par ty or its Agents or recorded in agreed minutes following oral disclosure and any other info rmation otherwise made available by the Disclosing Party or its Agents to the Receiving Party or its Agents, whether before, on or after the date of this Agreement, and whether in writing or otherw ise, including any information, analysis or specifications derived from, containing or reflecting such information but excluding information which:- a) is publicly available at the time of its disclosure or becomes publicly available (other than as a result of disclosure by the Receiving Party or any of its Agents contrary to the terms of this Agreement); or b) was lawfully in the possession of the Receiving Party or its Agents (as can be demonstrated by its written records or other reasonable evidence) fr ee of any restriction as to its use or disclosure prior to its being so disclosed; or
perturbation.changed_text: 1.2 “Proprietary Data” means any information or other data relating to one party (the “Disclosing Party”) and/or the business carried on or proposed or intended to be carried on by that party and which is made available for the purposes of the Proposal to the other party (the “Receiving Party”) or its Agents by the Disclosing Party or its Agents or recorded in agreed minutes following oral disclosure and any other information otherwise made available by the Disclosing Party or its Agents to the Receiving Party or its Agents, whether before, on or after the date of this Agreement, and whether in writing or otherwise, including any information, analysis or specifications derived from, containing or reflecting such information but excluding information which:- a) is publicly available at the time of its disclosure or becomes publicly available (other than as a result of disclosure by the Receiving Party or any of its Agents contrary to the terms of this Agreement); or b) was lawfully in the possession of the Receiving Party or its Agents (as can be demonstrated by its written records or other reasonable evidence) fr ee of any restriction as to its use or disclosure prior to its being so disclosed; or
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Proprietary Data' in section 1.2 creates an in-text contradiction because the contract continues to use 'Confidential Information' throughout the document. Section 2.1 refers to “All Confidential Information” thus creating confusion and ambiguity in enforcement as to whether the clause refers to Proprietary Data or something else. The definition of “Confidential Information” is only valid within section 1.2, and not outside of it. Thus rendering the term useless within the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 All Confidential Information given by one party to th is Agreement (the “Disclosing Party”) to the other party (the “Receiving Party”) will be treated by the Receiving Party as secret and confidential and will not, without the Disclosing Pa rty’s written consent, directly or indirectly communicate or disclose (whether in writing or orally o r in any other manner) Confidential Information to any other person other than in accord ance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Proprietary Data' in section 1.2 creates a direct conflict with section 2.1, which refers to 'All Confidential Information'. This misaligned terminology introduces ambiguity and uncertainty, as it's unclear whether section 2.1 now refers to 'Proprietary Data' or some other undefined category of information. Therefore, the change creates a strong in-text contradiction as described in the explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 1.4 “Information ” means all information in whatever form including, wi thout limitation, any information relating to systems, operations, plans, in tentions, market opportunities, know-how, trade secrets and business affairs whether in writing, conveyed orally or by machine-readable medium; and
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4 “Data” means all information in whatever form including, without limitation, any information relating to systems, operations, plans, intentions, market opportunities, know-how, trade secrets and business affairs whether in writing, conveyed orally or by machine-readable medium; and
perturbation.explanation: By changing the term 'Information' to 'Data' specifically in section 1.4, while the rest of the contract still uses the term 'Information' specifically in section 3.1, an in-text contradiction is introduced. Clause 3.1 states that 'The Receiving Party agrees to ensure proper and secure sto rage of all Information and any copies thereof.' This generates doubt regarding whether the term encompasses 'Data' as defined within clause 1.4. Or if 'Information' should be treated separately. This inconsistency leads to difficulties in contract enforcement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 The Receiving Party agrees to ensure proper and secure sto rage of all Information and any copies thereof.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Information,' which is then used consistently in section 3.1, stating that 'The Receiving Party agrees to ensure proper and secure storage of all Information'. The changed text redefines 'Information' as 'Data' in section 1.4. This introduces a contradiction because section 3.1 still uses 'Information.' This raises the question of whether the obligation to store 'Information' also includes 'Data' now, or if these are two distinct terms with different obligations. The change creates ambiguity and potential conflict in enforcement regarding which term applies to the storage obligation as defined in section 3.1. Since 'Information' has been replaced by 'Data' in the definition, it directly conflicts with 3.1.

perturbation.original_text: 2.2 The Receiving Party will only use the Confidential Infor mation for the sole purpose of technical and commercial discussions between the parties in relati on to the Proposal or for the subsequent performance of any contract between the parties in relat ion to the Proposal.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.2 The Receiving Party will only use the Proprietary Details for the sole purpose of technical and commercial discussions between the parties in relation to the Proposal or for the subsequent performance of any contract between the parties in relation to the Proposal.
perturbation.explanation: In Section 2.2, 'Confidential Information' is replaced with 'Proprietary Details'. This change creates a contradiction because 'Confidential Information' is defined elsewhere in the contract, while 'Proprietary Details' is not. The contract now lacks a clear definition for 'Proprietary Details,' making the scope of permitted use unclear and contradicting the defined usage of 'Confidential Information' in earlier sections. Section 2.1 is expecting the original term that was defined in the definitions.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.1 All Confidential Information given by one party to th is Agreement (the “Disclosing Party”) to the other party (the “Receiving Party”) will be treated by the Receiving Party as secret and confidential and will not, without the Disclosing Pa rty’s written consent, directly or indirectly communicate or disclose (whether in writing or orally o r in any other manner) Confidential Information to any other person other than in accord ance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The change in Section 2.2 replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Proprietary Details'. Section 2.1 explicitly defines how 'Confidential Information' should be treated. Because 'Proprietary Details' is not defined elsewhere in the contract, the Receiving Party's obligations regarding this new term are unclear and potentially contradictory to their obligations regarding 'Confidential Information'. This creates a direct conflict and ambiguity regarding which information is protected under the confidentiality terms, satisfying the conditions for a strong in-text contradiction as defined by 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 3 RECORDS AND RETURN OF INFORMATION 3.1 The Receiving Party agrees to ensure proper and secure sto rage of all Information and any copies thereof. 3.2 The Receiving Party shall keep a written record, to be sup plied to the Disclosing Party upon request, of the Confidential Information provided and a ny copies made thereof and, so far as is reasonably practicable, of the location of such Confi dential Information and any copies thereof. 3.3 The Company shall, within 7 (seven) days of receipt of a written demand from Transnet: a) return all written Confidential Information (including all copies); and b) expunge or destroy any Confidential Information from an y computer, word processor or other device whatsoever into which it was copied, read o r programmed by the Company or on its behalf.
perturbation.changed_text: 3 RECORDS AND RETURN OF INFORMATION 3.1 The Receiving Party agrees to ensure proper and secure sto rage of all Data and any copies thereof. 3.2 The Receiving Party shall keep a written record, to be sup plied to the Disclosing Party upon request, of the Sensitive Data provided and a ny copies made thereof and, so far as is reasonably practicable, of the location of such Confi dential Information and any copies thereof. 3.3 The Company shall, within 7 (seven) days of receipt of a written demand from Transnet: a) return all written Confidential Information (including all copies); and b) expunge or destroy any Confidential Information from an y computer, word processor or other device whatsoever into which it was copied, read o r programmed by the Company or on its behalf.
perturbation.explanation: In Section 3, 'Information' is replaced with 'Data' in clause 3.1, and 'Confidential Information' is replaced with 'Sensitive Data' in clause 3.2. Clause 3.3 however, still uses 'Confidential Information'. This creates an internal contradiction because the return and destruction obligations in 3.3 now only explicitly apply to 'Confidential Information', while the storage and record-keeping requirements apply to 'Data' and 'Sensitive Data'. The Receiving Party might argue that they are only obligated to return 'Confidential Information' as defined, and not other types of data. Also considering we changed the definition of Data in the definitions, its only valid there, and not outside of it. Its very inconsistent.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3 RECORDS AND RETURN OF INFORMATION 3.1 The Receiving Party agrees to ensure proper and secure sto rage of all Data and any copies thereof. 3.2 The Receiving Party shall keep a written record, to be sup plied to the Disclosing Party upon request, of the Sensitive Data provided and a ny copies made thereof and, so far as is reasonably practicable, of the location of such Confi dential Information and any copies thereof. 3.3 The Company shall, within 7 (seven) days of receipt of a written demand from Transnet: a) return all written Confidential Information (including all copies); and b) expunge or destroy any Confidential Information from an y computer, word processor or other device whatsoever into which it was copied, read o r programmed by the Company or on its behalf.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction because clause 3.1 uses 'Data', clause 3.2 uses 'Sensitive Data', while clause 3.3 still uses 'Confidential Information'. This misalignment of terminology creates a conflict within the document. The obligation to return and destroy in 3.3 only explicitly applies to 'Confidential Information', creating ambiguity regarding the handling of 'Data' and 'Sensitive Data'. This contradicts the original intention of section 3, which was to cover all information, and aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 8 PRIVACY AND DATA PROTECTION 8.1 The Receiving Party undertakes to comply with South A frica’s general privacy protection in terms of the Bill of Rights (Section 14) in connection wit h this Proposal and shall procure that its personnel shall observe the provisions of such Act (as applicable) or any amendments and re- enactments thereof and any regulations made pursuant the reto. 8.2 The Receiving Party warrants that it and its Agents ha ve the appropriate technical and organisational measures in place against unauthorised o r unlawful processing of data relating to the Proposal and against accidental loss or destructi on of, or damage to such data held or processed by them.
perturbation.changed_text: 8 PRIVACY AND DATA PROTECTION 8.1 The Receiving Party undertakes to comply with South A frica’s general privacy protection in terms of the Bill of Rights (Section 14) in connection wit h this Proposal and shall procure that its staff shall observe the provisions of such Act (as applicable) or any amendments and re- enactments thereof and any regulations made pursuant the reto. 8.2 The Receiving Party warrants that it and its Representatives ha ve the appropriate technical and organisational measures in place against unauthorised o r unlawful processing of data relating to the Proposal and against accidental loss or destructi on of, or damage to such data held or processed by them.
perturbation.explanation: In Section 8, 'personnel' is changed to 'staff' in Clause 8.1, but then in Clause 8.2 'Agents' is changed to 'Representatives'. The terms here are being changed out without and clear definitions, and thus the original intention of what should be used is confused. Clause 8.2 is expecting the original term that was previously defined.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8 PRIVACY AND DATA PROTECTION 8.1 The Receiving Party undertakes to comply with South A frica’s general privacy protection in terms of the Bill of Rights (Section 14) in connection wit h this Proposal and shall procure that its staff shall observe the provisions of such Act (as applicable) or any amendments and re- enactments thereof and any regulations made pursuant the reto. 8.2 The Receiving Party warrants that it and its Representatives ha ve the appropriate technical and organisational measures in place against unauthorised o r unlawful processing of data relating to the Proposal and against accidental loss or destructi on of, or damage to such data held or processed by them.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation involves misaligned terminology. The original text used 'personnel' and 'Agents.' The changed text replaces these with 'staff' and 'Representatives,' respectively. The contradicted text includes both of these new terms ('staff' and 'Representatives'). Because the terms are changed without clear definitions, this creates an in-text contradiction. The document now refers to both 'Agents' and 'Representatives' and 'personnel' and 'staff' seemingly interchangeably, without specifying whether they refer to the same parties or have different meanings, leading to uncertainty regarding who is bound by the obligations.
